choices: Enthusiastically supportive, arguing English should be made the world's official language
Strongly critical, arguing English imperialism has destroyed world cultures
Balanced, acknowledging both the problems of linguistic inequality and the benefits of a shared language
Indifferent, presenting facts without any evaluative commentary
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor presents critics who warn of linguistic inequality alongside those who note that a shared language facilitates cooperation — a deliberately balanced stance presenting multiple perspectives.
wrongReasons: The professor is analytical, not an advocate for making English an official world language.
The professor notes suppression of local languages as a historical fact but does not characterize it in purely condemnatory terms.
The professor clearly includes evaluative perspectives by citing critics and defenders of English's spread.

That English will eventually be replaced by a more logical constructed language

Cultural exchange versus economic opportunity is not the implied point.

lectureQuestions.text: What are the two students primarily trying to accomplish in this conversation?
choices: Deciding how to organize and present their research findings
Writing the research paper they will present
Practicing their verbal delivery in front of each other
Arguing about which research topic to choose
lectureQuestions.explanation: The students spend the conversation discussing the structure, order, and visual design of their presentation slides, not writing the paper itself or rehearsing delivery.
wrongReasons: The research paper has already been written; they are planning the presentation.
Verbal delivery practice is not described; they focus on slide structure.
Their topic is already decided; they are planning how to present it.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Planning a Research Presentation
passage.body:
Student A: Okay, we have about twenty minutes of presentation time. How do we want to structure the slides?
Student B: I think we should open with our research question — make it really clear what we were trying to find out before we get into method or results.
Student A: Agreed. And then methodology next? Or go straight to results?
Student B: Methodology first — the audience needs to understand how we collected data before the results will mean anything to them.
Student A: Good point. What about the results section — should we use graphs or tables?
Student B: Graphs, definitely. Visual data is easier to read quickly during a presentation. We can put the full tables in the appendix if people want the details.
Student A: That makes sense. And the conclusion — how long?
Student B: Two or three slides max. We want to end clearly: restate the main finding, discuss implications, and suggest future research directions.
Student A: Should we add a Q and A slide at the end?
Student B: Yes, always. It signals to the audience that we welcome questions and it gives us a moment to breathe before they start asking.

lectureQuestions.text: Why does Student B prefer using graphs over tables in the results section?
choices: Graphs contain more data than tables and are therefore more accurate
Graphs are easier for an audience to interpret quickly during a presentation
Tables are not permitted in academic presentations
Their professor specifically requested graphs instead of tables
lectureQuestions.explanation: Student B explicitly says 'Visual data is easier to read quickly during a presentation,' making speed and clarity the reason for preferring graphs over tables.
wrongReasons: The preference is about clarity and speed, not data quantity or accuracy.
No rule against tables in presentations is mentioned; tables are offered as an appendix option.
No professor requirement is cited; the decision is the students' own.

Strongly in favor, viewing it as both a signal to the audience and a personal benefit
Uncertain, wanting to check with the professor before deciding
lectureQuestions.explanation: Student B says 'Yes, always,' indicating strong support, and gives two reasons: it signals openness to questions and gives the presenters a moment to collect themselves.
wrongReasons: Student B is clearly in favor of the Q&A slide, not opposed to it.
Student B says 'always,' not 'optional.' The stance is definitive.
No mention of consulting the professor is made.

lectureQuestions.text: What is the central argument of this lecture?
choices: Sleep deprivation impairs the brain's ability to regulate emotional responses
Getting more than eight hours of sleep leads to emotional numbness
Dreaming is the most important function of sleep for emotional health
Emotional problems are the primary cause of sleep disorders
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor's central argument is that inadequate sleep disrupts emotional regulation, specifically through its effects on the amygdala and prefrontal cortex.
wrongReasons: The professor discusses insufficient sleep, not excessive sleep.
Dreaming is not mentioned; the focus is on sleep quantity and brain function.
The lecture describes sleep deprivation causing emotional problems, not the reverse.

passage.title: Lecture: The Role of Sleep in Emotional Regulation
passage.body: Professor: We tend to think of sleep as passive — a period of inactivity when the body simply rests. But neuroscience has revealed that the sleeping brain is extraordinarily active, and one of its most critical jobs is processing and regulating emotion. At the center of this is a small almond-shaped structure called the amygdala, which is responsible for detecting threats and triggering fear and anger responses. When we are well-rested, the prefrontal cortex — the rational, decision-making part of the brain — keeps the amygdala in check. But after even one night of poor sleep, studies show the amygdala becomes up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli. The prefrontal cortex essentially goes offline, losing its ability to moderate the emotional alarm system. This is why sleep-deprived people are more irritable, more anxious, and more prone to emotional outbursts. The implications extend beyond bad moods. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to anxiety disorders, depression, and impaired empathy. If you're struggling emotionally and also sleeping poorly, the relationship between those two facts is probably not coincidental.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the professor, what happens to the amygdala after one night of poor sleep?
choices: It shrinks in size, reducing emotional responses
It becomes up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli
It temporarily shuts down, causing emotional numbness
It begins producing excess cortisol, leading to chronic stress
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states that studies show the amygdala becomes 'up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli' after even one night of poor sleep.
wrongReasons: The amygdala becomes more reactive, not smaller or less reactive.
The amygdala does not shut down; rather it becomes hyperreactive when the prefrontal cortex goes offline.
Cortisol production is not mentioned in the lecture.

lectureQuestions.text: Why does the student come to office hours?
choices: To discuss a failing grade and find out how to improve
To request a makeup exam after missing a test
To appeal a grade they believe was marked incorrectly
To drop the course before the deadline
lectureQuestions.explanation: The student tells the professor they received a failing grade and want to understand what went wrong and how to do better going forward.
wrongReasons: A makeup exam is not requested; the student is asking about improving future performance.
The student does not dispute the grade as an error; they want to understand it and improve.
Dropping the course is never mentioned.

passage.title: Office Hours: Discussing a Failing Grade
passage.body:
Student: I got a fifty-two on the midterm and I'm really worried. I studied for hours but I don't know what went wrong.
Professor: I'm glad you came in. A lot of students don't, and that's a missed opportunity. Let me pull up your exam. Looking here, I can see the main problem isn't your knowledge of the content — your short answers are actually pretty solid. The issue is with the essay question. You listed a lot of relevant facts, but you didn't construct an argument.
Student: What do you mean by that?
Professor: In an academic essay, facts alone aren't enough. You need a clear thesis — a central claim — and then your evidence has to build toward proving that claim. Your essay read more like a list than an argument.
Student: I think I see. How do I fix that?
Professor: Before you write a single sentence, write your thesis down. Then ask yourself: does each paragraph support this specific claim? If a paragraph doesn't connect to your thesis, cut it or rework it. Come see me with a draft outline before the final exam and I'll give you feedback.
Student: That would really help. Thank you.
Professor: Of course. One bad exam doesn't define the course. There's still plenty of opportunity.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the professor, what was the specific weakness in the student's exam essay?
choices: It contained factual errors about the course content
It listed facts without constructing a central argument or thesis
It was too short to adequately address the question
It was written in an informal style inappropriate for academic work
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor identifies the problem as structural: the student listed relevant facts but did not construct an argument built around a clear thesis — the essay 'read more like a list than an argument.'
wrongReasons: The professor says the short answers showed solid content knowledge; the facts in the essay were described as relevant, not wrong.
Essay length is not discussed as a problem.
Writing style or formality is not mentioned as an issue.

passage.title: Memory and Learning: What the Research Shows
passage.body: Good morning, everyone. Today we're going to explore what cognitive psychology and neuroscience have taught us about memory — how it works, how it can fail, and most importantly, what strategies can help you learn more effectively. Memory consolidation is the process by which short-term memories become stable long-term memories. This happens largely during sleep, when the brain replays recently acquired information, strengthening the connections between neurons. One of the most robust findings in learning research is the spacing effect: distributing your study sessions over time leads to far better retention than cramming. Related to this is retrieval practice — actively recalling information from memory is more effective than re-reading, because the act of retrieval itself strengthens the memory trace. Another powerful strategy is interleaving: mixing different topics or subjects during a single study session, rather than studying one topic exhaustively before moving to the next. Research on stress shows that moderate, acute stress can temporarily enhance memory consolidation by activating systems in the hippocampus — the brain region central to memory. However, chronic stress damages the hippocampus and impairs memory formation. Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve demonstrates that without review, we forget most new information rapidly. Regular self-testing and spaced review counteract this curve. Ultimately, memory is not a fixed resource: deliberate strategies can significantly enhance how much and how long we remember.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the lecture, what is memory consolidation?

The ability to recall information immediately after learning it
A technique used to improve study efficiency
The process of forgetting unnecessary information
lectureQuestions.explanation: The lecturer defines consolidation as the stabilization of short-term memories into long-term storage.
wrongReasons: Immediate recall is short-term retrieval, not consolidation.
Study techniques are applications, not the definition.
Forgetting is the opposite process.

lectureQuestions.text: What is the main topic of this lecture?
choices: The causes and consequences of rapid urbanization globally

passage.title: Urbanization: Drivers, Challenges, and Opportunities
passage.body: Good afternoon. Today's lecture examines one of the defining trends of the twenty-first century: rapid urbanization. More than half of the world's population now lives in cities, and this proportion is projected to reach two-thirds by 2050. The primary driver of urbanization is economic opportunity. Cities concentrate jobs, higher wages, and access to services like healthcare and education, drawing millions from rural areas. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. Cities struggle to provide adequate housing, infrastructure, and services for expanding populations. When formal housing cannot keep pace with growth, informal settlements — commonly called slums — emerge on city peripheries, often lacking clean water, sanitation, and reliable electricity. Environmental pressures also intensify: increased industrial activity and transportation raise air and water pollution, while the urban heat island effect makes cities significantly warmer than surrounding areas due to built surfaces absorbing heat. Despite these challenges, urbanization also creates opportunities. Economies of scale allow cities to deliver healthcare, education, and public transport more efficiently than dispersed rural populations. Innovation and entrepreneurship tend to cluster in cities. Smart city technologies — sensors, data analytics, and integrated digital services — offer tools for managing urban complexity. The key to sustainable urbanization lies in integrated urban planning: anticipating growth, coordinating housing with transport and public services, and ensuring that economic gains reach all residents, including the poorest. Urbanization is inevitable; the question is whether it can be managed wisely.

lectureQuestions.text: What does the lecturer identify as the primary driver of urbanization?
choices: Economic opportunity, including jobs and higher wages in cities
Government policies that restrict rural settlement
lectureQuestions.explanation: Economic opportunity — jobs and higher wages — is cited as the primary pull factor drawing people to cities.
wrongReasons: Policies may influence patterns but are not the primary driver.
Climate displacement is a factor in some cases but not the primary global driver.
Infrastructure follows economic development rather than driving it.

Environmental pressures also intensify: increased industrial activity and transportation raise air and water pollution, while the urban heat island effect makes cities significantly warmer than surrounding areas due to built surfaces absorbing heat. Despite these challenges, urbanization also creates opportunities. Economies of scale allow cities to deliver healthcare, education, and public transport more efficiently than dispersed rural populations. Innovation and entrepreneurship tend to cluster in cities. Smart city technologies — sensors, data analytics, and integrated digital services — offer tools for managing urban complexity. The key to sustainable urbanization lies in integrated urban planning: anticipating growth, coordinating housing with transport and public services, and ensuring that economic gains reach all residents, including the poorest. Urbanization is inevitable; the question is whether it can be managed wisely.

lectureQuestions.text: What does the lecturer mean by the term 'urban heat island'?
choices: The phenomenon where cities are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas due to built surfaces and human activity.
A type of urban park designed to provide cooling in hot climates.
A government program to reduce energy consumption in cities.
A climate zone found only in tropical coastal cities.
lectureQuestions.explanation: The urban heat island effect occurs because concrete, asphalt, and human activity absorb and retain heat.
wrongReasons: It is not a park.
It is a naturally occurring phenomenon, not a government program.
It occurs in cities worldwide, not only tropical coastal ones.

passage.title: Urbanization: Drivers, Challenges, and Opportunities
passage.body: Good afternoon. Today's lecture examines one of the defining trends of the twenty-first century: rapid urbanization. More than half of the world's population now lives in cities, and this proportion is projected to reach two-thirds by 2050. The primary driver of urbanization is economic opportunity. Cities concentrate jobs, higher wages, and access to services like healthcare and education, drawing millions from rural areas. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. Cities struggle to provide adequate housing, infrastructure, and services for expanding populations. When formal housing cannot keep pace with growth, informal settlements — commonly called slums — emerge on city peripheries, often lacking clean water, sanitation, and reliable electricity. Environmental pressures also intensify: increased industrial activity and transportation raise air and water pollution, while the urban heat island effect makes cities significantly warmer than surrounding areas due to built surfaces absorbing heat. Despite these challenges, urbanization also creates opportunities. Economies of scale allow cities to deliver healthcare, education, and public transport more efficiently than dispersed rural populations. Innovation and entrepreneurship tend to cluster in cities. Smart city technologies — sensors, data analytics, and integrated digital services — offer tools for managing urban complexity. The key to sustainable urbanization lies in integrated urban planning: anticipating growth, coordinating housing with transport and public services, and ensuring that economic gains reach all residents, including the poorest. Urbanization is inevitable; the question is whether it can be managed wisely.

lectureQuestions.text: What solution to urban challenges does the lecturer emphasize?
choices: Integrated urban planning that anticipates growth and coordinates housing, transport, and services.
Limiting migration into cities through legal restrictions.
Reducing economic activity in cities to slow population growth.
Relocating urban populations back to rural areas.
lectureQuestions.explanation: The lecturer emphasizes proactive, coordinated planning as the key to managing urban growth sustainably.
wrongReasons: Migration restrictions address symptoms without solving infrastructure needs.
Reducing economic activity would harm livelihoods.
Mass relocation is neither practical nor desirable.

Cities concentrate jobs, higher wages, and access to services like healthcare and education, drawing millions from rural areas. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. When formal housing cannot keep pace with growth, informal settlements — commonly called slums — emerge on city peripheries, often lacking clean water, sanitation, and reliable electricity. lectureQuestions.text: How does the lecturer describe the relationship between urbanization and poverty?
choices: Urbanization can reduce poverty through economic opportunity but also concentrate poverty in informal settlements.
Urbanization always eliminates poverty by raising wages for all residents.
Urban poverty is less severe than rural poverty in all cases.
Poverty is unrelated to urbanization rates.
lectureQuestions.explanation: The lecturer presents urbanization as both a path out of poverty and a cause of concentrated urban poverty.
wrongReasons: Not all urban residents benefit equally from economic growth.
The lecture acknowledges urban poverty in slums.
Urbanization and poverty are closely related.

lectureQuestions.text: What does the lecturer say about smart city technology?
choices: It can improve efficiency of services and infrastructure management in growing cities.
It is unaffordable for any developing nation.
Smart city technology has replaced the need for urban planning.
It primarily benefits the wealthiest residents of cities.
lectureQuestions.explanation: Smart city technology — sensors, data analytics, digital services — is presented as a tool for managing urban complexity.
wrongReasons: Some developing cities are adopting smart city approaches affordably.
Technology supports but does not replace planning.
The benefits are intended to be broadly shared.

Cities concentrate jobs, higher wages, and access to services like healthcare and education, drawing millions from rural areas. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. lectureQuestions.text: What demographic trend does the lecturer highlight as driving urbanization?
choices: The movement of younger, working-age populations from rural to urban areas.
The retirement of older populations to city centers.
The movement of wealthy families from cities to suburbs.
A decline in birth rates in urban areas.
lectureQuestions.explanation: Young working-age people seek economic opportunity in cities, driving urbanization.
wrongReasons: Retirement to cities is not the primary driver.
Wealthy suburban movement is the opposite trend in many developed countries.
Birth rates in cities are typically lower than in rural areas.

passage.title: Urbanization: Drivers, Challenges, and Opportunities
passage.body: Good afternoon. Today's lecture examines one of the defining trends of the twenty-first century: rapid urbanization. More than half of the world's population now lives in cities, and this proportion is projected to reach two-thirds by 2050. The primary driver of urbanization is economic opportunity. Cities concentrate jobs, higher wages, and access to services like healthcare and education, drawing millions from rural areas. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. lectureQuestions.text: What overall conclusion does the lecturer reach about urbanization?
choices: Urbanization is an inevitable and potentially beneficial trend that requires careful management.
Urbanization should be halted to preserve traditional ways of life.
Cities are inherently unsustainable and cannot support large populations.
Rural areas offer better living conditions than cities in all measurable ways.
lectureQuestions.explanation: The lecturer views urbanization as unstoppable and potentially positive with good planning.
wrongReasons: The lecturer accepts urbanization as inevitable rather than calling for it to stop.
The lecture discusses how cities can be made sustainable.
Rural advantages and urban advantages vary; no blanket comparison is made.

passage.title: Urbanization: Drivers, Challenges, and Opportunities
passage.body: Good afternoon. Today's lecture examines one of the defining trends of the twenty-first century: rapid urbanization. More than half of the world's population now lives in cities, and this proportion is projected to reach two-thirds by 2050. The primary driver of urbanization is economic opportunity. This rural-to-urban migration is predominantly a movement of younger, working-age populations seeking better livelihoods. However, rapid urbanization brings significant challenges. Smart city technologies — sensors, data analytics, and integrated digital services — offer tools for managing urban complexity. textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Scarce fits because the passage describes resource shortages driving conflict, requiring a word meaning insufficient in supply.
wrongReasons: Abundant means plentiful, the opposite of shortage.
Invisible refers to something unseen, not to supply levels.
Ancient refers to age, not availability.

passage.title: Resource Conflicts
passage.body: Throughout human history, competition over natural resources has driven some of the most destructive conflicts. When populations grow faster than the land can support, or when droughts reduce agricultural yields for consecutive seasons, tensions between neighboring communities escalate rapidly. Historians and political scientists note that wars fought over territory are often, at their root, struggles over water, fertile soil, or minerals. Modern conflicts in arid regions confirm this pattern. As climate change intensifies, analysts warn that freshwater will become increasingly ___ in many parts of the world, heightening the risk of future disputes.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Inherited fits because the passage discusses traits passed genetically from parents to offspring.
wrongReasons: Purchased implies buying, which does not apply to biological traits.
Invented means created anew, not transmitted biologically.
Borrowed implies temporary acquisition, inappropriate for genetic transmission.

passage.title: Genetic Traits
passage.body: Genetics has transformed our understanding of why individuals differ so greatly in physical appearance, susceptibility to disease, and even aspects of personality. Deoxyribonucleic acid, the molecule that encodes biological instructions, is passed from parent to child at conception. Traits such as eye color, blood type, and height are determined largely by the combination of genetic material each parent contributes. While environment and nutrition also play significant roles in development, the foundational blueprint is ___ from one generation to the next through the sequence of nucleotides in each person's genome.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Declined fits because the passage describes falling biodiversity and species loss over time.
wrongReasons: Doubled means increased, contradicting the loss described.
Stabilized implies no change, inconsistent with ongoing decline.
Scattered refers to distribution, not to population or count changes.

passage.title: Biodiversity Loss
passage.body: The variety of life on Earth represents billions of years of evolutionary history, yet scientists warn that we are living through one of the planet's most severe extinction events. Human activities including deforestation, pollution, and the introduction of invasive species have placed unprecedented pressure on ecosystems. Many species that were common a century ago are now critically endangered, and some have disappeared entirely. Conservation organizations monitor population counts of vulnerable animals to track changes over time. According to recent assessments, the populations of wild vertebrates have ___ by more than two-thirds since the 1970s, signaling a biodiversity crisis.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Enforce fits because the passage describes governments ensuring compliance with laws through legal mechanisms.
wrongReasons: Ignore means disregard, the opposite of the governmental role described.
Propose means to suggest, not to implement or compel.
Discuss means to talk about, not to apply rules authoritatively.

passage.title: Rule of Law
passage.body: Democratic societies depend on the principle that no individual or institution stands above the law. This concept, often called the rule of law, requires that legal rules apply equally to all citizens regardless of wealth, social status, or political connections. Courts serve as the primary institutions responsible for interpreting legislation and resolving disputes. Police and regulatory agencies act on behalf of the state to ensure that individuals comply with established rules. Without credible institutions empowered to ___ legal standards, laws risk becoming symbolic documents rather than binding obligations that shape actual behavior.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Volatile fits because the passage discusses rapidly changing, unpredictable market conditions.
wrongReasons: Stable means steady and unchanging, the opposite of the market behavior described.
Distant refers to physical or temporal separation, not market character.
Colorful is informal and refers to variety of hue, unrelated to financial markets.

passage.title: Financial Markets
passage.body: Investing in financial markets carries inherent risks that even experienced analysts struggle to quantify. Stock prices respond to an enormous range of signals: corporate earnings reports, central bank interest rate decisions, geopolitical events, and shifts in consumer sentiment. Because so many factors interact simultaneously, prices can swing dramatically in very short periods. Investors who expect steady, predictable returns are often surprised by sudden downturns that erase months of gains in a single trading day. Economists describe such environments as ___, cautioning that past performance during calm periods provides little guarantee of future outcomes when conditions change rapidly.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Migration fits because the passage describes populations moving from one region to another in search of better conditions.
wrongReasons: Irrigation refers to supplying water to crops, unrelated to population movement.
Celebration refers to festive events, not demographic shifts.
Legislation refers to law-making, not the movement of people.

passage.title: Urban Growth
passage.body: Cities around the world have expanded rapidly over the past two centuries, driven by industrialization and the promise of economic opportunity. Rural populations have relocated to urban centers in search of employment, education, and access to healthcare. This shift has transformed landscapes, created megacities of tens of millions of residents, and placed enormous pressure on housing and infrastructure. Demographers track these patterns carefully, noting that the movement is not always voluntary. Climate change, armed conflict, and collapsing agricultural systems have turned internal ___ into a defining feature of twenty-first century societies, reshaping the political and cultural makeup of receiving regions.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Observe fits because the passage describes astronomers watching and studying celestial phenomena.
wrongReasons: Destroy is physically impossible and contradicts the scientific purpose described.
Avoid means to keep away from, inconsistent with the act of scientific study.
Rename refers to changing labels, not to studying phenomena.

passage.title: Telescope Technology
passage.body: Advances in telescope design have repeatedly expanded the boundaries of human knowledge about the universe. From Galileo's first crude lens to the giant radio dishes and space-based observatories of today, each technological generation has revealed phenomena invisible to previous instruments. Infrared telescopes pierce dust clouds that block visible light, exposing star-forming regions deep within galaxies. Gravitational wave detectors sense ripples in spacetime produced by colliding black holes billions of light-years away. With every new instrument, astronomers gain the ability to ___ phenomena that were previously beyond detection, continually revising our models of how the cosmos formed and evolves.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Expand fits because the passage describes economies and cities growing larger through trade and investment.
wrongReasons: Compress means to reduce in size, the opposite of growth.
Freeze implies no change, contradicting the dynamic growth described.
Dissolve means to break apart, not to grow or develop.

passage.title: Trade and Prosperity
passage.body: International trade has been a driver of economic development for millennia, allowing societies to specialize in goods they produce most efficiently and exchange them for products they cannot easily make themselves. Ancient trade routes connected civilizations across deserts and oceans, spreading not only commodities but also ideas, languages, and technologies. In the modern era, containerized shipping and digital communication have reduced transaction costs dramatically, enabling small businesses to reach global customers. When barriers to trade are lowered, markets tend to ___, giving consumers access to a greater variety of goods at lower prices and stimulating innovation through increased competition.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Chronic fits because the passage describes long-lasting, persistent health conditions that require ongoing management.
wrongReasons: Brief means short in duration, the opposite of long-term illness.
Optional implies choice, inappropriate for describing disease conditions.
External refers to something outside the body, not to duration or persistence.

passage.title: Managing Long-Term Illness
passage.body: Healthcare systems around the world are grappling with the rising burden of non-communicable diseases that persist over many years and require continuous medical attention. Conditions such as diabetes, hypertension, and asthma affect hundreds of millions of people globally, reducing quality of life and placing enormous financial pressure on health services. Unlike infectious diseases that resolve once the pathogen is eliminated, these illnesses do not simply disappear after a course of treatment. Patients must monitor symptoms, adjust medications, and make lasting lifestyle changes. Physicians refer to such conditions as ___ because they endure for months or years and cannot be cured by a single intervention.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Vocabulary fits because the passage discusses the set of words a language learner must acquire to communicate effectively.
wrongReasons: Dialect refers to a regional variety of a language, not to word acquisition.
Accent refers to pronunciation patterns, not the set of words known.
Grammar refers to structural rules, not the store of words.

passage.title: Language Acquisition
passage.body: Children acquire their first language with remarkable speed and without formal instruction, typically mastering the basic sound system by age three and constructing grammatically complex sentences by age five. Researchers in developmental linguistics attribute this capacity partly to innate cognitive structures that are primed to detect patterns in speech. Adults learning a second language follow a different trajectory, often struggling most not with pronunciation or syntax but with the sheer quantity of words they must internalize. Studies consistently show that knowing the two thousand most common words in a target language allows a learner to understand roughly eighty percent of spoken conversation, underscoring the central role of ___ in achieving communicative fluency.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Dormant fits because the passage describes a volcano that is temporarily inactive but not extinct.
wrongReasons: Active means currently erupting or showing activity, contradicting the temporary quiet described.
Shallow refers to depth, not to volcanic activity status.
Narrow refers to width, not to a volcano's eruptive state.

passage.title: Volcanic Hazards
passage.body: Volcanoes represent one of the most dramatic expressions of the Earth's internal energy. When tectonic plates collide or pull apart, magma can force its way to the surface, releasing gases, ash, and lava across vast areas. Geologists classify volcanoes according to their recent eruptive history. An active volcano has shown signs of unrest within the past ten thousand years, while an extinct volcano has not erupted in recorded history and is unlikely to do so again. Between these categories lies a third type: a volcano that is currently quiet but retains the potential to erupt in the future. Such a volcano is described as ___, and its apparent calm should not be mistaken for permanent inactivity.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Ratify fits because the passage describes the formal process by which governments approve and make treaties legally binding.
wrongReasons: Draft means to prepare an initial version, which precedes the approval stage described.
Oppose means to resist, contradicting the act of official approval.
Publicize means to make widely known, not to formally adopt into law.

passage.title: International Treaties
passage.body: International agreements between sovereign states follow a complex process before they acquire legal force. Diplomats first negotiate the terms, often over months or years, producing a draft text that must satisfy multiple parties with competing interests. Once negotiators reach consensus, their governments must decide whether to accept the final document. In many countries, this requires parliamentary approval, because treaties create binding obligations that affect domestic law and national resources. The United States Constitution, for example, requires a two-thirds majority in the Senate before the government can ___ any international agreement, ensuring that major foreign policy commitments reflect broad legislative support rather than executive action alone.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Symmetry fits because the passage discusses how balanced, mirror-image features signal biological fitness in mate selection.
wrongReasons: Disorder refers to lack of organization, the opposite of the balanced trait described.
Color is a separate trait discussed in evolutionary biology, not the one tied to bilateral balance.
Weight refers to mass, not to the balanced proportions discussed.

passage.title: Evolutionary Aesthetics
passage.body: The standards of physical attractiveness that humans apply when choosing partners are not arbitrary cultural inventions. Evolutionary psychologists argue that preferences for certain features reflect signals of underlying genetic health and developmental stability. Across cultures, people consistently rate certain facial and bodily proportions as attractive, suggesting a biological basis for these judgments. One widely studied signal is bilateral balance: the degree to which the left and right sides of the face and body mirror each other precisely. Developmental disruptions caused by disease, parasites, or nutritional deficiencies tend to disturb this balance. As a result, greater ___ is interpreted by potential mates as evidence of strong genetic quality and resistance to environmental stressors.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Nutrients fits because the passage describes the essential substances plants absorb from soil to support growth.
wrongReasons: Toxins are harmful substances, the opposite of what plants require for healthy growth.
Gases refers to airborne substances, while the passage focuses on soil absorption.
Signals refers to information, not to chemical substances used in plant metabolism.

passage.title: Soil Health
passage.body: Healthy soil is far more than a passive medium in which plant roots anchor themselves. It is a living ecosystem teeming with bacteria, fungi, earthworms, and countless other organisms that interact in ways scientists are still working to fully understand. These organisms decompose organic matter, releasing chemical compounds that plants can absorb through their root systems. The health of a crop depends heavily on the biological and chemical richness of the surrounding soil. Intensive farming practices that rely on synthetic chemical inputs can degrade this ecosystem over time, reducing the soil's capacity to supply essential ___ that plants need to grow, flower, and produce food.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Transparency fits because the passage describes open government practices that allow citizens to see how decisions are made.
wrongReasons: Censorship means restricting information, the opposite of openness.
Rivalry refers to competition, not to open government practices.
Inflation refers to rising prices, unrelated to governmental accountability.

passage.title: Open Government
passage.body: Democratic accountability rests on citizens' ability to scrutinize the actions of their elected representatives. When governments conduct their affairs behind closed doors, corruption becomes easier to conceal, and public resources can be misused without consequence. Many countries have therefore enacted freedom of information laws that require agencies to release records of their deliberations, expenditures, and decision-making processes upon request. Advocates argue that this access is not merely a bureaucratic formality but a fundamental precondition for genuine democracy. When governments operate with ___, citizens, journalists, and civil society organizations can identify abuses, hold officials accountable, and participate meaningfully in public life.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Erode fits because the passage describes how repeated waves gradually wear away coastal rock and land.
wrongReasons: Strengthen means to make more solid, the opposite of coastal wearing away.
Preserve means to keep intact, contradicting the process of gradual loss described.
Measure means to determine size, not to cause physical change.

passage.title: Coastal Erosion
passage.body: Coastlines are among the most dynamic environments on Earth, shaped by the constant interaction between land and sea. Waves deliver enormous kinetic energy each time they break against a shoreline, slowly wearing away rock faces, redistributing sand, and carving distinctive landforms such as sea arches and sea stacks. The rate at which this process occurs depends on the hardness of the underlying rock, the frequency of storm events, and the height of the tidal range. Human activities including seawall construction and sand dredging can disrupt natural sediment transport, accelerating the tendency of waves to ___ unprotected sections of coastline at rates far exceeding historical averages.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Confidence fits because the passage describes how successful performance experience builds a student's belief in their own abilities.
wrongReasons: Anxiety refers to worry and stress, the opposite of the positive psychological outcome described.
Patience refers to tolerance of delay, not to self-belief built through success.
Creativity refers to imaginative thinking, not to the self-assurance gained from achievement.

passage.title: Academic Self-Efficacy
passage.body: Educational psychologists have long recognized that a student's belief in their own ability to succeed is one of the most powerful predictors of academic performance. This belief, which researchers call self-efficacy, is not fixed at birth but develops through experience. When students attempt challenging tasks and succeed, they receive powerful evidence that their efforts produce results. Teachers who design tasks at the right level of difficulty allow students to experience these small victories regularly. Over time, a history of successful performances accumulates into a deep sense of academic ___, enabling students to approach new challenges with persistence rather than avoidance, even when initial attempts fail.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Proliferate fits because the passage describes misinformation spreading and multiplying rapidly across digital platforms.
wrongReasons: Diminish means to decrease, the opposite of the spreading described.
Consolidate means to combine into one, not to spread widely.
Alternate means to switch between options, not to multiply or spread.

passage.title: Digital Misinformation
passage.body: The architecture of social media platforms creates conditions that favor the rapid spread of false and misleading content. Algorithms designed to maximize engagement tend to surface emotionally provocative material, regardless of its accuracy, because outrage and surprise drive higher click and share rates than measured factual reporting. Once a false claim enters a network, it can reach millions of users before fact-checkers have time to evaluate it. Corrective information, when it does appear, tends to reach far fewer people and generates less emotional response. Researchers studying information ecosystems note that falsehoods consistently tend to ___ faster than verified facts, posing a significant challenge for democratic discourse.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Attributed fits because the passage describes assigning the cause of a historical event to specific factors.
wrongReasons: Devoted means dedicated, which does not convey causal assignment.
Converted means changed in form, not used to describe causal explanation.
Restricted means limited in scope, not related to identifying causes.

passage.title: Historical Causation
passage.body: Explaining why major historical events occur is one of the central challenges of the discipline. Historians resist single-cause explanations because complex events almost always result from the intersection of multiple forces operating across different timescales. The collapse of a civilization, for example, might involve climatic changes that reduce agricultural output, elite mismanagement that exhausts fiscal reserves, and external military pressure applied at a moment of internal weakness. Scholars debate how much weight each factor deserves. The fall of the Western Roman Empire has been ___ to dozens of causes ranging from lead poisoning and pandemic disease to military overstretch and the gradual erosion of civic participation.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Accelerate fits because the passage describes how feedback loops cause warming to happen faster than initial projections suggest.
wrongReasons: Suppress means to slow down or stop, the opposite of what feedback loops do.
Redirect means to change direction, not to increase the rate of a process.
Detect means to identify or discover, not to influence the rate of change.

passage.title: Climate Feedback Loops
passage.body: Climate scientists warn that global warming is not a simple linear process in which adding more greenhouse gases produces proportionally more heat. Instead, warming triggers secondary effects that themselves generate additional warming, creating self-reinforcing cycles known as feedback loops. As Arctic sea ice melts, it exposes darker ocean water that absorbs more solar radiation than the reflective ice surface it replaced. As permafrost thaws, it releases stored carbon dioxide and methane, adding to the atmospheric burden. passage.title: Trade and National Wealth

passage.title: Balancing Present and Future Needs

passage.title: When Learned Behaviors Disappear

passage.title: Counting vs. Measuring

passage.title: Dominance Without Force

passage.title: Drug Interactions

passage.title: Explaining the Whole by Its Parts

passage.title: Crossing Thresholds

passage.title: Cultural Diffusion

passage.title: Brain Hemispheres

passage.title: UN General Assembly Resolutions

passage.title: Gothic Structural Innovation
passage.body: The great cathedrals of the High Middle Ages represent some of the most ambitious engineering achievements of the premodern world. Gothic architects pursued unprecedented heights and luminosity, replacing the thick, solid walls of Romanesque construction with slender stone piers and vast windows filled with colored glass. This ambition created a structural problem: pointed vaults exerted powerful outward forces on the supporting walls that threatened to push them apart. Builders solved this by constructing external masonry arches that reached from a free-standing pier outside the building to the top of the nave wall, transferring the outward thrust over the side aisles and safely into the ground. This ingenious element, called the flying ___, became the defining structural signature of Gothic architecture.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

passage.title: Demographic Transition
passage.body: Demographers describe a pattern that has repeated across industrializing societies: as improvements in sanitation, nutrition, and medicine cause death rates to fall faster than birth rates can adjust, populations grow rapidly for a generation or more before eventually stabilizing. This transition has reshaped the distribution of humanity across the globe over the past two centuries. One of its most visible consequences is urban growth. As rural families shed excess labor to industrial cities, and as natural increase adds to the urban pool, cities experience a ___ in population that infrastructure rarely keeps pace with, producing the overcrowded, underserviced megacities that characterize much of the developing world today.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

wrongReasons: Degrade means to worsen or break down, the opposite of restoration.

Acidify means to lower pH, not to restore nutrient content.

passage.title: Soil Nutrient Cycles
passage.body: Agricultural productivity depends on the cycling of key elements—nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and trace minerals—through soil, plants, and organic matter. Each harvest removes a portion of these elements from the field, since the nutrients bound in grain and fiber leave with the crop rather than returning to the soil as they would in an undisturbed ecosystem. Continuous cultivation without intervention therefore depletes the chemical reserves available to subsequent crops, reducing yields over time. Farmers have historically addressed this through the application of animal manure, crop rotation with nitrogen-fixing legumes, and fallow periods that allow natural biological processes to ___ the soil's nutrient reserves before the next growing season.

passage.title: Research Design in Psychology
passage.body: Establishing causal relationships in psychology is considerably more difficult than simply observing that two variables are correlated. When researchers find that children who spend more time reading score higher on vocabulary tests, they cannot conclude that reading causes the improvement without ruling out alternative explanations. Wealthier households may provide both more books and better nutrition, both of which independently affect cognitive development. textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Attenuate fits because the passage describes how ocean circulation reduces the severity of temperature extremes in coastal regions by moderating heat, which is a precise fit for the word meaning to reduce intensity.

passage.title: Oceanic Climate Regulation
passage.body: The world's oceans play a critical role in regulating surface temperatures across vast distances by absorbing heat in summer and releasing it slowly through autumn and winter. Because water has a high specific heat capacity, it changes temperature far more slowly than land surfaces under equivalent solar radiation. Coastal regions therefore experience far smaller seasonal and diurnal temperature swings than continental interiors at the same latitude. Cities located on windward coasts may remain mild in winter and cool in summer because prevailing winds carry marine air over land. This moderating effect tends to ___ temperature extremes, making coastal environments more hospitable for agriculture and human settlement than their geographic latitude alone would suggest.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Curation fits because the passage describes the editorial selection and organization of content by gatekeepers, which is the precise function described—choosing what to surface to audiences rather than allowing all content equal exposure.
wrongReasons: Virality refers to the organic spread of content by users, not to editorial selection.

Aggregation refers to collecting content from multiple sources, not to applying editorial judgment in selecting it.

passage.title: Algorithmic Gatekeeping
passage.body: The transition from print to digital media has profoundly altered who decides which information reaches large audiences. Newspaper editors once served as explicit gatekeepers, selecting among competing stories based on criteria of newsworthiness they could articulate and defend. Readers received a finite, deliberately chosen set of items each day. Digital platforms replaced this editorial function with algorithmic systems that rank and filter content based on predicted engagement rather than journalistic judgment. Critics argue that algorithmic ___ systematically disadvantages accurate but emotionally unremarkable content in favor of sensational material, because the signals used to train recommendation systems reflect user behavior rather than informational value. The result is an information environment shaped by engagement metrics rather than editorial responsibility.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

Allegorical means using symbolic narrative, describing technique not linguistic medium.

passage.title: Medieval Literature
passage.body: Throughout the medieval period, Latin served as the dominant language of scholarship, religious practice, and formal written culture across Europe. Most texts that survive from the early Middle Ages were composed by clerics for an educated minority capable of reading the classical tongue. Beginning in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, however, a significant shift occurred: writers increasingly composed romances, epics, and lyric poetry in the everyday spoken languages of their regions. Dante's Divine Comedy in Tuscan Italian, Chaucer's Canterbury Tales in Middle English, and the anonymous Nibelungenlied in Middle High German represent landmark examples of this movement. Scholars describe these works as ___ literature, celebrating their role in elevating spoken languages to vehicles of serious artistic and philosophical expression.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

The passage explicitly notes that commitments fall short of preventing all damage.
wrongReasons: Eliminate implies complete removal of harm, but the passage emphasizes that current pledges are insufficient for full prevention.

passage.title: Climate Diplomacy
passage.body: International climate negotiations have produced a succession of agreements that commit signatory states to reducing greenhouse gas emissions on specified timescales. The Paris Agreement of 2015 marked a significant achievement in diplomatic terms, establishing a framework through which countries submit nationally determined contributions and undergo periodic review. However, analysts at leading climate research institutions have consistently found that even full implementation of existing pledges would leave the world on a trajectory toward warming well above the 1.5-degree threshold identified as critical. The agreements therefore serve primarily to ___ the worst projected consequences of climate disruption rather than to prevent them entirely, buying time for deeper structural transformations in energy systems that current political conditions have not yet made possible.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Corroborated fits because the passage describes new archaeological findings that support and confirm earlier hypotheses proposed on indirect evidence, requiring a word meaning to strengthen or verify an existing claim.

Supplanted means replaced, implying the new findings overturned the hypothesis rather than supported it.

passage.title: Migration Archaeology
passage.body: For decades, archaeologists debated whether the spread of the Corded Ware cultural complex across Neolithic Europe represented genuine population movement or merely the diffusion of pottery styles and burial practices among settled communities. The question seemed unlikely to be resolved definitively given the fragmentary nature of the material record. Ancient DNA analysis has transformed this debate. Genomic studies of skeletal remains from multiple European sites reveal a dramatic shift in ancestry approximately five thousand years ago, consistent with large-scale migration from the Pontic-Caspian steppe. These findings ___ the steppe migration hypothesis that linguists and archaeologists had proposed on circumstantial grounds, providing molecular evidence that population displacement, rather than cultural borrowing alone, drove the change.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Circumscribe fits because the passage describes peremptory norms of international law as constraints that limit the range of actions states may legally take, requiring a word meaning to restrict within defined limits. The passage emphasizes limitation of authority rather than its destruction or fragmentation.
wrongReasons: Erode means to gradually wear away, implying a progressive weakening rather than a defined legal boundary.
Consolidate means to combine or strengthen, the opposite of limiting state authority.
Fragment means to break into pieces, implying disintegration rather than a principled legal constraint.

passage.title: Jus Cogens Norms
passage.body: International law operates primarily through the voluntary consent of sovereign states, which retain broad discretion over their domestic and foreign policies. However, a category of norms recognized as jus cogens occupies a special hierarchical position: these are peremptory principles from which no derogation is permitted by treaty or custom. The prohibition on genocide, the ban on torture, and the rules against slavery represent the core examples acknowledged in contemporary jurisprudence. States cannot contract out of these obligations even through bilateral agreement. Jus cogens norms therefore ___ the domain of sovereign discretion by establishing an outer boundary of conduct that international law treats as non-negotiable regardless of political circumstances or the expressed preferences of the parties involved.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

wrongReasons: Compressive strength refers to resistance to crushing forces, not to stretching—cables experience tension, not compression.

Shear strength refers to resistance to sliding forces acting perpendicular to the material axis, not to the along-axis tension in cables.

passage.title: Suspension Bridge Engineering
passage.body: The suspension bridge achieves its remarkable spans through an elegant transfer of forces. The deck, carrying the weight of traffic and its own structure, hangs from vertical suspender cables attached to two main cables that sweep in a catenary curve between tall towers. The main cables then anchor into massive concrete blocks at each end of the bridge, transferring the load into the ground. The critical property demanded of the steel used in these main cables is extraordinary resistance to stretching forces: as load increases, the material must lengthen without fracturing. Engineers specify steel with very high ___ strength for cable applications, ensuring that the cables can sustain the enormous pulling forces generated by the bridge's dead load and live traffic without yielding.

wrongReasons: Progressive means higher-income earners pay a higher share, the opposite of the described structure.

Neutral implies no distributional effect, whereas the passage explicitly describes an unequal burden falling on lower earners.

passage.title: Tax Incidence
passage.body: Public finance economists analyze not merely the statutory rate at which taxes are levied but the ultimate distribution of the tax burden across income groups after behavioral adjustments and price changes are accounted for. Sales taxes, excise duties, and value-added taxes are often cited as examples where formal and effective incidence diverge from apparent neutrality. Because lower-income households spend a higher proportion of their earnings on consumption and save relatively little, they contribute a larger fraction of their income to consumption-based levies than wealthier households who direct more of their earnings into savings and investment. A tax structured this way is described as ___, since the effective rate falls as income rises rather than remaining constant or increasing.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Desistance fits because the passage describes the process by which individuals cease offending behavior over time, which is the criminological term for the gradual withdrawal from criminal activity. The passage asks about the mechanism of stopping crime rather than preventing or punishing it.
wrongReasons: Recidivism means relapsing into criminal behavior, the opposite of ceasing offending.

passage.title: Life-Course Criminology
passage.body: Criminologists studying criminal careers have long recognized that offending behavior is not randomly distributed across the life course. Most individuals who commit crimes do so during adolescence and early adulthood, with rates declining sharply as people move into their late twenties and thirties. Researchers debate what drives this pattern. Some emphasize biological maturation; others point to the stabilizing effects of employment, marriage, and parenthood, which provide stakes in conventional life that make criminal behavior increasingly costly. The process by which offenders gradually cease their criminal activity as they age and acquire social bonds is called ___, and understanding its mechanisms has become a central focus for practitioners seeking alternatives to incarceration as the primary response to crime.

wrongReasons: Autonomy refers to respecting patient self-determination, not to the obligation to avoid causing harm.

passage.title: Bioethical Principles
passage.body: Modern bioethics draws on a framework of four foundational principles developed by Tom Beauchamp and James Childress in their influential work Principles of Biomedical Ethics. Each principle addresses a distinct dimension of the ethical relationship between clinician and patient. The principle most directly concerned with avoiding harm to patients occupies a pivotal place in clinical decision-making: when a proposed treatment carries significant risk of side effects or complications, physicians must weigh whether those risks are justified by the anticipated benefits. Hippocratic tradition encapsulated this obligation in the injunction 'first, do no harm.' Contemporary bioethics labels this duty ___, distinguishing it from the positive obligation to promote welfare and treating it as a constraint on interventions even when patient benefit is the stated goal.

passage.title: Adolescent Brain Development
passage.body: The human brain continues to develop well into early adulthood, with some regions reaching structural maturity only in the mid-twenties. Among the most consequential maturational processes that occur during adolescence is the progressive sheathing of nerve fibers with a fatty substance called myelin. This insulating layer dramatically increases the speed at which electrical signals travel along axonal pathways, allowing different brain regions to coordinate their activity more rapidly and efficiently. The prefrontal cortex, which governs impulse control, planning, and evaluation of long-term consequences, is among the last regions to complete this process. wrongReasons: Sovereignty refers to a state's supreme authority over its territory, not to the procedural right to bring a claim.
Jurisdiction refers to a court's authority to hear a case, not a party's right to appear as a claimant.
Immunity refers to protection from legal proceedings, the opposite of the right to initiate them.

passage.title: Access to International Courts
passage.body: The International Court of Justice, as the principal judicial organ of the United Nations, resolves legal disputes between states in accordance with international law. However, not every state that believes it has been wronged may simply file a case and expect it to be heard on the merits. A preliminary determination must be made as to whether the applicant state has a sufficient legal interest in the subject matter of the dispute to justify bringing the claim before the court. States that are merely affected in a general political sense, without a specific legally cognizable interest in the matter, may have their applications dismissed before any examination of the underlying facts. This threshold requirement, known as ___, ensures that the court's docket is occupied by genuine legal controversies between parties with direct interests at stake.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

passage.title: Fracture Mechanics
passage.body: Materials scientists classify the failure behavior of solids along a spectrum that has fundamental consequences for engineering design. At one end, ductile materials such as mild steel deform significantly before fracturing, providing visible warning of impending failure and absorbing large amounts of energy in the process. At the other end of the spectrum, certain ceramics, glasses, and cast irons fail in a very different manner: they absorb little energy, show almost no plastic deformation prior to fracture, and crack catastrophically with minimal warning. Engineers must account for this characteristic when selecting materials for structural applications, particularly those subjected to impact loading or low temperatures, which can induce even normally ductile metals to behave in a ___ fashion.

wrongReasons: Multipolarity describes a system with several roughly equal great powers, not one dominant actor.

passage.title: International Order
passage.body: The structure of the international system—the distribution of power among its major actors—has varied considerably across historical periods and carries significant consequences for the stability and character of relations between states. When one state possesses such a disproportionate share of economic, military, and institutional power that it can shape the rules governing international trade, security arrangements, and diplomatic norms, scholars describe the resulting order as one of ___, with the dominant state functioning as a system manager whose preferences are reflected in global institutions. textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

Deductive reasoning moves from general premises to a necessary conclusion, whereas the passage describes selecting among competing explanations under uncertainty.

passage.title: Scientific Inference
passage.body: Philosophers of science distinguish several forms of reasoning that scientists employ in moving from evidence to conclusions. The mode most characteristic of clinical medicine, forensic investigation, and historical reconstruction involves encountering a puzzling or surprising phenomenon and then selecting among available hypotheses the explanation that would, if true, most economically account for all the observed evidence. A physician examining a constellation of symptoms reasons in this way when she settles on a diagnosis: she cannot achieve deductive certainty, nor does she have enough similar cases to generalize inductively. Instead, she reasons to the best available explanation given the information at hand. This mode of inference is called ___ reasoning, and it captures how scientists often work at the frontier of knowledge where data are incomplete and multiple explanations remain live possibilities.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
textCompletion.explanation: Crowd out fits because the passage describes the mechanism by which government borrowing competes with private borrowers for available funds, raising interest rates and reducing private investment—the precise economic phenomenon known as crowding out.
wrongReasons: Subsidize means to provide financial support, not to compete for and displace private access to credit.

passage.title: Fiscal and Monetary Interaction
passage.body: The economic consequences of large-scale government borrowing extend beyond the direct impact of fiscal deficits on the public debt stock. When the government enters capital markets to sell bonds, it competes with private firms and households who are simultaneously seeking to borrow funds for investment and consumption. If the supply of savings available in the economy is limited, increased government demand for credit drives up the interest rate—the price of borrowing—making it more expensive for private entities to finance investment projects. Higher borrowing costs cause some private investment that would have been profitable at lower rates to become unviable. Economists warn that large fiscal deficits may therefore ___ private investment, partially or wholly offsetting the direct stimulative effect of government spending.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

Polyphonic is broader and does not imply the rule-governed independence described.
wrongReasons: Polyphonic simply means having multiple voices or parts, without implying the rigorous rule-governed independence of melodic lines that characterizes counterpoint.
Homophonic describes music in which one main melody is supported by chordal accompaniment, the opposite of independent equal voices.
Monophonic means a single unaccompanied melody, with no independent parts at all.

passage.title: Baroque Composition
passage.body: The music of Johann Sebastian Bach represents the apex of a compositional tradition that prioritized the independent movement of distinct melodic voices over harmonic simplicity. Rather than writing a single prominent tune supported by chords, Bach wove together two, three, or four entirely independent melodic lines, each following its own path while simultaneously conforming to strict rules governing how the voices could interact. Intervals of a second or seventh required careful preparation and resolution; parallel perfect consonances between any two voices were forbidden. This exacting discipline produced music of extraordinary complexity and structural coherence. Analysts describe Bach's fugues and inventions as masterpieces of ___ writing, in which the horizontal independence of each voice takes precedence over the vertical relationships that govern later harmonic styles.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:

wrongReasons: Pandemic refers to disease spreading across multiple countries or continents, not to stable regional baseline presence.

passage.title: Disease Ecology
passage.body: Epidemiologists categorize the patterns of disease occurrence using a vocabulary that distinguishes between fundamentally different dynamics. A disease that circulates continuously within a defined geographic population, maintaining a relatively stable and predictable rate of occurrence year after year without requiring repeated external introduction, occupies a distinct category from outbreaks that arrive suddenly, burn through a susceptible population, and then subside. Malaria in sub-Saharan Africa provides the most cited example of the former pattern: it does not disappear between transmission seasons but persists in the human reservoir and mosquito population alike, generating a predictable annual burden. Epidemiologists describe such diseases as ___, reflecting their settled, self-sustaining presence in a community rather than their explosive spread.

textCompletion.text: Choose the word that best completes the passage:
